Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
  "opinion": "I've found that managing conversations around healthcare topics, especially something as sensitive as COVID vaccines, can be tricky even for someone like me who is just a regular person working in retail. My personality from my Big Five scores shows I'm high on extraversion but low on agreeableness and openness – basically, I enjoy talking to people, including those online friends I have, but I don't necessarily bend over backwards to please everyone or try super hard to fit into new situations.\n\nIn our family, we're quite diverse culturally because my parents went through different things; one has a bachelor's degree in finance from back home, and the other earned their master's abroad. Growing up with that mix made me understand early on that people see the world differently, so I'm not shocked when someone online argues about vaccines being dangerous or unnecessary.\n\nI think the best way to handle these discussions is to start by listening without judgment, then gently guide it toward facts and personal experiences if possible. For instance, a few months ago in our local community groups here in New York City – where I spend quite some time on social media daily – there was this heated debate about kids getting vaccinated during the pandemic.\n\nI shared my thoughts based on being single parent with one child who actually did get their shots and is fine now. My husband knows all about COVID protocols, so he helped me present it more professionally than I could alone. Being self-employed at 54 years old makes balancing personal opinions and professional advice a bit harder, but honestly, sometimes the most important thing is just being honest while staying respectful.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've found that managing conversations around healthcare topics, especially something as sensitive as COVID vaccines, can be tricky even for someone like me who is just a regular person working in retail. My personality from my Big Five scores shows I'm high on extraversion but low on agreeableness and openness – basically, I enjoy talking to people, including those online friends I have, but I don't necessarily bend over backwards to please everyone or try super hard to fit into new situations.

In our family, we're quite diverse culturally because my parents went through different things; one has a bachelor's degree in finance from back home, and the other earned their master's abroad. Growing up with that mix made me understand early on that people see the world differently, so I'm not shocked when someone online argues about vaccines being dangerous or unnecessary.

I think the best way to handle these discussions is to start by listening without judgment, then gently guide it toward facts and personal experiences if possible. For instance, a few months ago in our local community groups here in New York City – where I spend quite some time on social media daily – there was this heated debate about kids getting vaccinated during the pandemic.

I shared my thoughts based on being single parent with one child who actually did get their shots and is fine now. My husband knows all about COVID protocols, so he helped me present it more professionally than I could alone. Being self-employed at 54 years old makes balancing personal opinions and professional advice a bit harder, but honestly, sometimes the most important thing is just being honest while staying respectful.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
- Start with listening without judgment
- Then gently guide toward facts and personal experiences
- Use family diversity to understand different perspectives
- Listen first in online debates like those on social media
response_parsed.tone: Conversational but firm, informative yet approachable.
